authors: Darshan Deshpande, Varun Gangal, Hersh Mehta, Anand Kannappan, Rebecca Qian, Peng Wang
title: MemTrack: Evaluating Long-Term Memory and State Tracking in Multi-Platform Dynamic Agent Environments
venue: NeurIPS 2025 Workshop: Scaling Environments for Agents (SEA)
storage_format: NA — benchmark agnostic to the backend; evaluates Mem0 (ChromaDB, embeddings) and Zep (temporal knowledge graph) as memory components
agent_framework: NA — evaluates agents with GPT-5 and Gemini-2.5-Pro + multi-platform tools (Slack, Linear, Git)
llm_used: GPT-5, Gemini-2.5-Pro, GPT-4o-mini (embeddings for Mem0), GPT-4o (LLM-judge)
benchmarks_used: MemTrack (47 curated instances, multi-platform: Slack, Linear, Git in a dockerized environment)
performance_metrics: Correctness (LLM-judge), Efficiency (tool call count), Redundancy (tool call overlap), tool call entropy
baseline_compared: LLM+NoMem, LLM+Mem0, LLM+Zep (for GPT-5 and Gemini-2.5-Pro)
main_result: Frontier LLMs such as GPT-5 reach only ~60% Correctness on MemTrack; memory components (Mem0, Zep) do not significantly improve performance and increase tool call redundancy (~21%). Performance degrades on follow-up questions.
performance_vs_baseline: GPT-5+Mem0 0.610 vs GPT-5+NoMem 0.601 (+1.5%); Gemini+Mem0 0.118 vs Gemini+NoMem 0.144 (-18%); Zep with no significant gain
limitations: Dataset limited to 47 instances; only two LLMs evaluated; Mem0 and Zep used with default configurations without optimization; simulated environment may not capture the full complexity of real workflows; no latency or cost evaluation
validity_threats: Only GPT-5 and Gemini-2.5-Pro tested; Mem0 and Zep with default configurations; LLM-as-judge (GPT-4o) for Correctness may introduce bias; simulated scenarios vs real enterprise workflows; 5 runs for statistical significance on a small dataset
